food_subgroup: Grilled eel rice plate — steamed white rice topped with kabayaki-style grilled freshwater eel with tare sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a complete unagi teishoku plate. FOODON_03302051 (eel food product) is the closest parent covering the primary protein component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers freshwater eel as a commodity. No composite dish code exists for unagi teishoku; closest_parent applied to the primary ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for unagi plate or grilled eel rice dish.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: A single portion unagi plate typically consists of approximately 100–120 g of kabayaki-grilled freshwater eel served over approximately 200–230 g of steamed white rice, totalling around 350 g per plate as served at Japanese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Unagi (Freshwater Eel), Kabayaki — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; Cooked White Rice — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; composite dish estimated by weighted ingredient proportion
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete unagi teishoku plate. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: (1) Fish, eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) representing kabayaki-style grilled eel at ~31% of plate weight (~110 g), and (2) Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (FDC ID 169756) representing steamed rice at ~69% of plate weight (~240 g). Tare sauce contribution to sodium and sugar is included as an additive estimate based on typical kabayaki tare composition (~15 g sauce per serving). Per-100g values are back-calculated from the composite 350 g serving estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (steamed, Japanese-style) GI ~54–56; composite dish GI estimated proportionally
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite unagi plate. The GI is estimated from the dominant glycemic contributor: steamed white rice (Japanese short-grain/long-grain), which has a GI of approximately 54–56 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. Composite GI set to 54 (low). Glycemic load per 350 g serving is calculated using an estimated available carbohydrate (net carb) of 42.5 g per serving (after fibre adjustment), resulting in GL = (54 × 42.5) / 100 = 22.95, rounded to 23.0. This is consistent with standard GL methodology.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tare sauce glaze
hypertension.guidance: A single unagi plate provides approximately 980 mg sodium per serving (280 mg/100 g × 350 g), representing nearly 43% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion frequency.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from white rice base and added sugar in tare sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: The unagi plate has a low GI of approximately 54 driven by the steamed white rice base and sugar-containing tare glaze; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, consider substituting with brown rice if available, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from freshwater eel offset by moderate sodium and saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Freshwater eel provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin D which are cardioprotective, but the sodium load from tare sauce and moderate saturated fat content (~3.5 g per serving) warrant moderation; consumption of one portion per week is unlikely to adversely affect cardiovascular risk in otherwise healthy individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Plate Single Portion is a Japanese dish consisting of kabayaki-style grilled freshwater eel (unagi) served over a bed of steamed white rice, typically presented as a single-serving set meal (teishoku). The eel is butterflied, skewered, steamed to remove excess fat, then grilled over charcoal or a gas flame and repeatedly basted with a sweet-savoury tare glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, producing a lacquered, caramelised exterior with a tender, rich interior. The dish is characterised by its deeply umami, slightly sweet flavour profile, silky-soft eel texture contrasting with the firm, sticky rice beneath. In Japan, unagi is traditionally eaten during the midsummer festival of Doyo no Ushi no Hi for its perceived stamina-boosting properties. In Singapore, unagi plates are served at Japanese restaurant chains such as Sukiya and at mixed Japanese food stalls. Nutritionally, freshwater eel is a notable source of vitamin D, vitamin B12, omega-3 fatty acids, and high-quality protein. The dish has a low glycemic index due to the white rice base and sugar in the tare sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated due to the soy-based glaze, which is a consideration for those with hypertension. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or soy allergies, and contains gluten from the wheat in soy sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in unagi plate?
Is unagi plate good for diabetics?
How much sodium in unagi plate?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers a complete unagi teishoku plate. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for grilled eel and cooked white rice, with an additive estimate for tare sauce sodium and sugar. The estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but not derived from a direct dish measurement, warranting medium confidence.
notes: Composite dish nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Fish, eel, mixed species, cooked dry heat (FDC ID 175136) and Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (FDC ID 169756), weighted at approximately 31% eel and 69% rice by plate weight (350 g total serving). Tare sauce sodium and sugar contribution estimated from typical kabayaki tare composition. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for composite unagi plate exists in approved sources. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the weighted USDA SR Legacy component values and are not independently verified for this composite dish. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy eel and rice entries. Dietary flags: halal status is not confirmed as mirin and sake (alcohol-containing ingredients) are used in tare sauce preparation; is_halal set to false accordingly. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are applied as closest_parent to the primary eel ingredient; no composite dish ontology code exists in either system. SNOMED CT has no applicable concept for this dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 54, as per University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI <= 55 is low).
Refined gi_source.source_match_notes to clarify the glycemic load calculation, explicitly stating the 'available carbohydrate (net carb)' used for GL derivation.
Updated health_context.type2_diabetes.key_factor and natural_language_description to reflect the corrected 'low' GI category.
